---
title: "Getting Started with Claude Code"
subtitle: "A Practical Guide for IPA Colombia Research Analysts"
author: "IPA Colombia MEL Team"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: true
    embed-resources: true
    theme: cosmo
    smooth-scroll: true
    code-copy: true
    link-external-newwindow: true
execute:
  echo: false
  eval: false
---

# Before You Start {#sec-prereqs}

This guide introduces Claude Code, an artificial intelligence (AI) coding assistant that runs inside the terminal. It is designed for Innovations for Poverty Action (IPA) Colombia research analysts who are proficient in Stata but have not used Visual Studio Code (VS Code) or Claude Code before. The guide takes 50 to 75 minutes to complete, including two hands-on exercises.

::: {.callout-note}
## Prerequisites

Before starting, confirm the following are in place:

- Stata 17 or later is installed and licensed on your machine
- Your project repository is cloned from GitHub (e.g., the SpC repo)
- Node.js 18 or later is installed ([download here](https://nodejs.org/))
- You have an Anthropic API key (ask your project lead if you do not have one)
:::

The guide progresses from orientation to practice. Sections 1 through 5 are reading. Section 6 bridges reading and doing. Sections 7 and 8 are hands-on exercises where you will use Claude Code on realistic Stata tasks.

---

# VS Code in Five Minutes {#sec-vscode}

## Why VS Code?

Most research analysts switch between the Stata graphical user interface (GUI), a file browser, and a terminal window throughout the day. VS Code puts all three into one screen. It is a free, open-source code editor maintained by Microsoft, and it is the environment where Claude Code runs.

## The Four Panels

VS Code organizes the workspace into four areas that research analysts use daily.

1. **Sidebar (file explorer)**: Shows the folder structure of your project. Click any file to open it in the editor. Toggle it with `Ctrl+B`.
2. **Editor**: The main area where files open as tabs. You can split the editor to view two files side by side.
3. **Integrated terminal**: A command-line interface built into VS Code. This is where Claude Code runs. Toggle it with `` Ctrl+` `` (the backtick key, above Tab).
4. **Extensions panel**: A marketplace for add-ons that enhance VS Code. Open it with `Ctrl+Shift+X`.

## Three Essential Actions

These three actions cover 90 percent of what a new user needs.

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Windows

| Action | Shortcut |
|--------|----------|
| Open a folder | `Ctrl+K`, then `Ctrl+O` |
| Open the terminal | `` Ctrl+` `` |
| Search across all files | `Ctrl+Shift+F` |

### macOS

| Action | Shortcut |
|--------|----------|
| Open a folder | `Cmd+O` |
| Open the terminal | `` Ctrl+` `` |
| Search across all files | `Cmd+Shift+F` |
:::

## Extensions to Install

Open the terminal in VS Code and run the following commands one at a time. These extensions add Stata syntax highlighting, Claude Code integration, and basic Git support.

```bash
code --install-extension kylebarron.stata-enhanced
code --install-extension anthropic.claude-code
code --install-extension eamodio.gitlens
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Getting comfortable

Do not worry about mastering VS Code before moving on. You will learn it by using it during the exercises. The three actions above are enough to get started.
:::

---

# What Is Claude Code? {#sec-what-is-cc}

Claude Code is an AI coding assistant built by Anthropic. It runs inside the VS Code terminal as a command-line tool. Unlike browser-based chatbots such as ChatGPT, Claude Code reads the files in your project folder, understands the project structure, and can write or edit code directly on your machine.

## How It Differs from Other AI Tools

Three features distinguish Claude Code from general-purpose AI chatbots.

1. **Project context**: Claude Code reads your project files automatically. It sees the folder structure, existing do-files, configuration files, and documentation. There is no need to copy-paste code into a chat window.
2. **Direct editing**: Claude Code can create, modify, and delete files in your project. It suggests changes and asks for your approval before making them.
3. **Project instructions**: Claude Code follows written instructions stored in a file called `CLAUDE.md` at the root of your project. These instructions tell it the project conventions, coding standards, and rules specific to your work.

## What Claude Code Can Do for Stata Work

- Write new do-files following IPA and DIME Analytics coding standards
- Debug errors by reading Stata log files
- Generate LaTeX tables using `esttab` or `outreg2` patterns
- Explain existing code in plain language
- Refactor do-files to improve readability or performance

## What Claude Code Cannot Do

- Access or read data that contains personally identifiable information (PII)
- Replace your judgment on research design or statistical methodology
- Run Stata by itself (it uses project commands such as `just stata-run` that launch Stata)
- Access Box, email, or other systems outside your local machine

::: {.callout-important}
## Data safety

Claude Code can read any file on your computer that you open in VS Code. Raw survey data often contains PII. Section 5 of this guide covers security rules in detail. Read it carefully before using Claude Code on any project with human subjects data.
:::

---

# Installation and First Run {#sec-install}

Follow these steps to install Claude Code and verify it works. The process takes about five minutes.

## Step 1: Install Claude Code

Open the VS Code terminal (`` Ctrl+` ``) and run:

```bash
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
```

::: {.callout-note}
If you see a permissions error on Windows, close VS Code and reopen it as Administrator. On macOS, prefix the command with `sudo`.
:::

## Step 2: Set Up Your API Key

Claude Code needs an Anthropic API key to function. Set it as an environment variable so it persists across sessions.

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Windows (PowerShell)

```powershell
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY", "sk-ant-your-key-here", "User")
```

Restart VS Code after running this command.

### macOS / Linux

Add the following line to your `~/.zshrc` or `~/.bashrc` file:

```bash
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-your-key-here"
```

Then restart your terminal.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Keep your API key private

Never paste your API key into a shared document, Slack message, or Git commit. The key is linked to your personal account and incurs usage charges. If you suspect it has been exposed, rotate it immediately on the [Anthropic Console](https://console.anthropic.com/).
:::

## Step 3: Launch Claude Code

Navigate to your project folder in the terminal and start Claude Code:

```bash
cd /path/to/your/project
claude
```

Claude Code reads the `CLAUDE.md` file in the project root on startup. You should see a greeting message and a prompt where you can type.

## Step 4: Verify It Works

Type the following question at the Claude Code prompt:

```
What project is this?
```

If Claude Code is working correctly, it will describe the project based on the `CLAUDE.md` file. For the SpC project, it should mention Sanar para Crecer 3.0, ICBF, and the Monitoring, Evaluation, and Learning (MEL) pipeline.

---

# How Claude Code Knows Your Project {#sec-project-context}

Claude Code does not start from zero each time. Three mechanisms give it persistent knowledge of your project: the `CLAUDE.md` file, the skills system, and project settings.

## CLAUDE.md: The Project Briefing

Every project that uses Claude Code has a `CLAUDE.md` file at its root. Think of it as the briefing document you would write for a new research analyst on their first day. It tells Claude Code what the project is, what rules to follow, and where to find things.

Here is a shortened excerpt from the SpC project's `CLAUDE.md`:

```yaml
# CLAUDE.md — SpC Project

## What This Project Is
SpC 3.0 = Sanar para Crecer 3.0 (HtG — "How to Grow")
A large-scale caregiver education program implemented by ICBF
in Colombia. The program includes an embedded RCT.

## Project-Specific Rules
- PII: Raw data contains PII — never read, process, or display raw data files.
- Do not use cap / capture except for cap mkdir.
- Global macros: Path globals are defined in 0_paths.do.
  Never redefine them in individual scripts.
```

This file is checked into the Git repository, so every team member shares the same project instructions. You do not need to edit it unless the project lead asks you to.

## Skills: The Reference Manuals

The `.claude/skills/` directory contains specialized instruction sets that give Claude Code domain expertise. Each skill is a markdown file with rules, examples, and conventions. The SpC project includes two main skills:

1. **Stata skill** (`.claude/skills/stata/SKILL.md`): Contains IPA and DIME Analytics coding standards, do-file header templates, path setup conventions, missing value handling, and the four-stage data flow (import, de-identify, clean, construct).
2. **IPA writing guidelines** (`.claude/skills/ipa-writing-guidelines/SKILL.md`): Contains style rules for all IPA written content, including paragraph structure, active voice requirements, acronym conventions, and citation format.

Claude Code loads the relevant skill automatically when the task matches. If you ask it to write a do-file, it applies the Stata skill. If you ask it to draft report text, it applies the writing guidelines.

**Mental model**: `CLAUDE.md` is the briefing for a new research analyst. Skills are the reference manuals on their desk.

## Settings and Permissions

The `.claude/settings.json` file controls which commands Claude Code can run without asking for your approval. In the SpC project, Stata commands through the `just` runner are pre-approved, so Claude Code can run scripts and read logs without prompting you each time.

::: {.callout-note}
You do not need to modify these files to use Claude Code. They are maintained by the project lead. Understanding that they exist helps explain why Claude Code "already knows" project conventions when you start a conversation.
:::

---

# Security and PII Rules {#sec-security}

This is the most important section in the guide. IPA projects routinely handle data that contains personally identifiable information, including names, national identification numbers (cedula), addresses, and phone numbers. Claude Code can read any file you open in VS Code. Careless use could expose PII to an external AI service.

## The Four Rules

::: {.callout-danger}
## Rule 1: Never paste PII into the Claude Code prompt

Do not type or paste names, cedula numbers, addresses, phone numbers, or any other identifying information directly into the Claude Code conversation. Claude Code sends your messages to Anthropic's servers for processing.
:::

::: {.callout-danger}
## Rule 2: Never ask Claude Code to read raw data files

Raw data files on the encrypted vault (D: drive) or in `data/raw/` directories contain PII. Do not ask Claude Code to open, read, summarize, or process these files. Claude Code should only work with do-files, logs, documentation, and cleaned output files that have been de-identified.
:::

::: {.callout-danger}
## Rule 3: Never ask Claude Code to display individual-level data

Even de-identified data can become re-identifiable when combined with enough detail. Do not ask Claude Code to show you "the first 10 observations" or "list all values of variable X." Ask it to write code that produces summary statistics instead.
:::

::: {.callout-danger}
## Rule 4: Review every file before committing

Before committing any file that Claude Code created or modified, review it to confirm that no PII leaked into code comments, variable labels, log output, or hard-coded values.
:::

## Safe versus Unsafe Scenarios

The following table illustrates the boundary between safe and unsafe requests.

| Scenario | Safe? | Why |
|----------|-------|-----|
| "Write a do-file that cleans survey data following this structure" | Safe | Claude Code writes code, does not see data |
| "Clean this survey data" | **Not safe** | Claude Code would need to read data that may contain PII |
| "Why is my merge failing? Here is the log" | Safe | Logs contain aggregates and error messages, not PII |
| "Show me the first 20 rows of the baseline dataset" | **Not safe** | Individual-level data may contain identifying information |
| "Debug this r(111) error in my do-file" | Safe | Claude Code reads the do-file code, not the data |
| "Summarize the attendance data for Maria Garcia in Centro Zonal Norte" | **Not safe** | Contains a name and location that identify an individual |

::: {.callout-tip}
## A simple test

Before sending a prompt, ask yourself: "Does this require Claude Code to see individual-level data or personal details?" If the answer is yes, rephrase your request to focus on the code rather than the data.
:::

---

# Prompt Engineering for Stata Tasks {#sec-prompts}

Claude Code responds better to some prompting styles than others. The five patterns below, each illustrated with a weak and a strong version, will help you get useful results on the first try.

## Pattern 1: Describe the Output, Not the Steps

Tell Claude Code what you need to produce, not how to build it step by step. It already knows Stata syntax.

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Weak Prompt

```
Use regress, then outreg2, then add a title, then save as .tex
```

### Strong Prompt

```
Run an intent-to-treat regression of FFMQ on treatment with strata fixed effects
and standard errors clustered at the UDS level. Export the table to LaTeX using
the outreg2 conventions documented in CLAUDE_DELIVERABLES_GUIDE.md.
```
:::

The strong prompt describes the analytical goal and references project conventions. Claude Code determines the Stata commands itself.

## Pattern 2: Reference Project Conventions

When a project has established patterns for tables, graphs, or file structure, point Claude Code to the relevant documentation.

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Weak Prompt

```
Make a nice table with the results
```

### Strong Prompt

```
Export the results using the esttab pattern in CLAUDE_DELIVERABLES_GUIDE.md.
Use the same column structure as the attrition tables in outputs/tables/.
```
:::

The strong prompt grounds Claude Code in existing project files rather than asking it to invent a format.

## Pattern 3: Provide Context About Errors

When debugging, give Claude Code the error message and surrounding context from the log file. Stata error codes alone are ambiguous.

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Weak Prompt

```
I got r(111), please fix
```

### Strong Prompt

```
I ran `just stata-script mel/2_AttritionAnalysis` and got r(111). Here are the
last 30 lines of the log:

[paste the relevant log excerpt here]

The error appears on the line that references `treatment_status`. I think the
variable may have been renamed upstream.
```
:::

The strong prompt gives Claude Code the log context, identifies the suspect line, and offers a hypothesis. Claude Code can investigate from there rather than guessing.

## Pattern 4: Ask for Explanations Alongside Code

When you want to learn from the code Claude Code produces, ask it to explain its choices.

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Weak Prompt

```
Write the merge command
```

### Strong Prompt

```
Write the merge command and explain why you chose 1:1 versus m:1 for this case.
Also explain what the assert results mean.
```
:::

This pattern is especially useful for analysts learning new Stata commands or statistical techniques.

## Pattern 5: Iterate, Do Not Start Over

After Claude Code produces an initial result, refine it with follow-up requests rather than rewriting the entire prompt.

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Weak Prompt

```
[rewrites the entire prompt from scratch with one small change]
```

### Strong Prompt

```
The table looks right. Add baseline controls as a second column and include
the F-test for joint significance at the bottom.
```
:::

Claude Code remembers the conversation context. Building on its previous output is faster and produces more consistent results than starting over.

---

# Exercise 1: Debug a Stata Error from a Log {#sec-exercise1}

**Estimated time: 10 to 15 minutes**

This exercise practices the most common task research analysts will use Claude Code for: diagnosing a Stata error from a log file.

## The Scenario

You ran an attrition analysis script and the log file shows an error. The relevant portion of the log is below.

```stata
. * ============================================================
. * Attrition Analysis — Treatment vs. Control
. * ============================================================

. use "$data/clean/masterMUESTRA_final_RCT.dta", clear

. * Define attrition variable
. gen attrited = (endline_survey == 0)

. * Run attrition regression
. regress attrited treatment_status i.strata, vce(cluster cod_uds)
variable treatment_status not found
r(111);

end of do-file

r(111);
```

## Your Task

1. **Open Claude Code** in your project folder (run `claude` in the VS Code terminal).
2. **Paste the log excerpt** into the Claude Code prompt and ask: "I got this r(111) error when running my attrition analysis. What is wrong and how do I fix it?"
3. **Read Claude Code's diagnosis.** It should identify that the variable `treatment_status` does not exist in the dataset and suggest checking the actual variable name.
4. **Follow up** by asking: "Can you check what treatment-related variables exist in the do-files in this project?"
5. **Evaluate the fix** that Claude Code proposes.

## What to Look For

- Does Claude Code correctly identify `r(111)` as a "variable not found" error?
- Does it suggest looking at the data dictionary or existing do-files to find the correct variable name?
- Does the proposed fix use the project's coding conventions (no `capture`, proper path globals)?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Reveal: Expected Diagnosis

The error occurs because the variable is named `treatment` (or `treat`), not `treatment_status`. This is a common pattern in research projects where variable names change during the cleaning stage but downstream scripts are not updated. Claude Code should suggest replacing `treatment_status` with the correct variable name found in the project's do-files or variable documentation. The fix should be a single line change in the do-file, not a workaround using `capture` or `rename`.
:::

---

# Exercise 2: Write a Descriptive Statistics Do-File {#sec-exercise2}

**Estimated time: 15 to 20 minutes**

This exercise practices the second most common task: asking Claude Code to generate a new analysis do-file from a specification.

## The Scenario

Your project lead asks you to produce a summary statistics table for early childhood development (ECD) worker outcome variables at endline, broken down by treatment and control groups. The table should follow the project's existing conventions.

## Your Task

### Step 1: Write the Initial Prompt

Open Claude Code and write a prompt based on this specification:

- **Dataset**: A clean, de-identified dataset of ECD worker outcomes
- **Variables**: `ffmq_total` (mindfulness score), `dass_stress` (stress score), `dass_anxiety` (anxiety score), `dass_depression` (depression score), `psi_total` (parenting stress index)
- **Breakdown**: Summary statistics by treatment arm (`treatment == 0` for control, `treatment == 1` for treatment)
- **Statistics**: Mean, standard deviation, number of observations, and a p-value from a t-test of difference
- **Output format**: LaTeX table using `esttab`, following the conventions in `CLAUDE_DELIVERABLES_GUIDE.md`
- **File location**: Save the do-file in the project's do-files directory

::: {.callout-note}
## Prompt hint

Remember Pattern 1: describe the output, not the steps. Tell Claude Code what table you need, what variables to include, and what format to use. Reference the project's deliverables guide for formatting conventions. You do not need to specify each Stata command.
:::

### Step 2: Review the Generated Do-File

After Claude Code produces the do-file, check it against this list:

- [ ] Does it include a proper header (project name, purpose, author, date)?
- [ ] Does it use `$data` or another path global instead of a hard-coded file path?
- [ ] Does it avoid using `capture` (except `cap mkdir`)?
- [ ] Does it handle extended missing values (`.d`, `.n`, `.r`, `.s`) correctly?
- [ ] Does the `esttab` call produce a clean LaTeX table?
- [ ] Does it save output to the `$tables` directory?

### Step 3: Iterate with Two Modifications

Ask Claude Code to make two changes to the do-file:

1. "Add a column showing the difference between treatment and control means, with the p-value from the t-test."
2. "Use the project's extended missing value conventions. Exclude `.d` (do not know) and `.r` (refused) from calculations but keep `.n` (not applicable) observations."

### Step 4: Evaluate the Iteration

Check whether Claude Code correctly modified the do-file without breaking the existing structure. A good result preserves the original table format while adding the requested columns and missing value handling.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Reveal: What a Strong Output Looks Like

A strong do-file from this exercise has these characteristics:

- Clean header with project metadata
- Path globals (`$data`, `$tables`) instead of hard-coded paths
- A loop over the outcome variables to avoid repetitive code
- `eststo` and `esttab` used together for the summary statistics table
- Extended missing values handled with explicit `if` conditions (e.g., `if !inlist(ffmq_total, .d, .r)`)
- Output saved as both `.tex` and `.csv` for flexibility
- No `capture` commands
- Comments that explain analytical decisions, not just what the code does
:::

---

# Quick Reference Card {#sec-reference}

## Common Claude Code Interactions

| What You Want | What to Type |
|---------------|-------------|
| Start Claude Code | `claude` (in the VS Code terminal, inside your project folder) |
| Ask a question | Type your question at the `>` prompt |
| Ask Claude Code to edit a file | "Edit the file at [path] to [change]" |
| Ask Claude Code to create a file | "Create a do-file that [specification]" |
| Run a Stata script | "Run `just stata-script mel/2_AttritionAnalysis` and tell me if it passes" |
| Debug an error | Paste the log excerpt and ask "What is wrong and how do I fix it?" |
| Exit Claude Code | Type `/exit` or press `Ctrl+C` |

## Safe versus Unsafe Patterns

| Safe to Ask | Never Ask |
|------------|-----------|
| "Write a do-file that..." | "Read the raw survey data and..." |
| "Debug this log output..." | "Show me observations where name = ..." |
| "Explain this Stata command..." | "Summarize the PII variables in..." |
| "Generate a LaTeX table for..." | "List all respondent names from..." |
| "Review this do-file for errors..." | "Open the file on the D: drive..." |

## Key Documentation Files

| File | What It Contains |
|------|-----------------|
| `CLAUDE.md` | Project overview, rules, and pipeline map |
| `documentation/CLAUDE_STATA_GUIDE.md` | How Claude Code runs Stata scripts |
| `documentation/CLAUDE_DELIVERABLES_GUIDE.md` | Table formatting, LaTeX pipeline, IPA brand identity |
| `.claude/skills/stata/SKILL.md` | IPA/DIME Stata coding standards |

---

# Next Steps {#sec-next}

Claude Code becomes more useful as you become more specific in your requests. Start with small, low-risk tasks and build up from there.

**Good first tasks after this guide:**

1. Ask Claude Code to explain an unfamiliar section of an existing do-file
2. Paste a Stata error from a log and ask for a diagnosis
3. Ask Claude Code to review a do-file you wrote for coding standard violations
4. Generate a simple summary statistics table for a presentation

**Resources for continued learning:**

- [Claude Code official documentation](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview) for the full feature set
- The project's `CLAUDE.md` file for project-specific conventions
- The `.claude/skills/` directory to understand what domain knowledge Claude Code has access to

::: {.callout-note}
## One final reminder

Claude Code is a tool, not a replacement for analytical judgment. Always review the code it generates, verify the statistical choices it makes, and confirm that outputs match your expectations before sharing results with your project lead or including them in deliverables.
:::
